paper_title: No Resource, No Benchmarks, No Problem? Evaluating and Improving LLMs for Code Generation in No-Resource Languages

research_equation.problem: LLMs cannot generate correct code for no-resource languages (0-1% pass@1 on hard tasks)
research_equation.root_cause: No training data → LLMs don't know the syntax → syntactic errors cause 2/3 of failures
research_equation.constraints: No benchmarks available, minimal training data (<3K repos), commercial tools don't support these languages
research_equation.goal: Enable companies to deploy cost-effective in-house code recommenders for proprietary languages

first_pass_title_abstract_intro.summary: Paper tackles code generation for no-resource languages (languages with virtually no training data). Builds 3 benchmarks translated to Gleam and MoonBit. Tests 6 LLMs. Key finding: further pre-training base model + weight diff transfer from instruct model achieves best results (up to 33% pass@1).
questions: How were the benchmarks validated for correctness?
Does the approach work for languages very different from any known language?
What's the computational cost of pre-training + weight diff?

second_pass_method_and_figures.summary: Nine languages across three resource levels. Three benchmarks (HumanEval, MBPP, McEval-Hard). Six LLMs (4 instruct, 2 base). Translation pipeline: ChatGPT + manual verification. Pre-training on all available code + docs; fine-tuning only on (doc, function) pairs. Weight diff computed between instruct and base models, then added to pre-trained base.
key_figures_understood: Table I: Language resource classification + GitHub repo counts
Table IV: Zero-shot pass@1 across 9 languages, 4 LLMs, 3 benchmarks — shows clear gradient
Table V: All 6 LLMs × techniques comparison — shows pre-training > fine-tuning > in-context learning
Table VI: Pre-training vs instruction transferring — shows +12% average improvement
Figure 1: Quality scores for fine-tuning datasets — median 5/5
gaps: No analysis of how performance correlates with syntactic similarity to high-resource languages
No experiment with constrained decoding as alternative to training
Table V doesn't show individual model run variance (only pass@1 estimated from 10 runs)

third_pass_deep_analysis.summary: Paper's key insight is the pre-train + weight-diff pipeline. This addresses the fundamental trade-off: pre-training teaches language knowledge but loses instruction following; weight diff restores it cheaply. The 8B model beating 32B is particularly striking — suggests the field may be over-investing in scale for niche applications.
unsupported_claims: MoonBit's 'AI-friendly design' claim is from the language authors themselves, not independently verified
third_pass_deep_analysis.future_work_connection: Constrained decoding (e.g., XGrammar) could be combined with pre-training for even better results — prevent syntactic errors at inference time

no_benchmarks.challenge: No existing benchmarks for no-resource languages (usually proprietary)
no_benchmarks.solution_module: Translation pipeline: ChatGPT generates translations → automated syntax check → manual verification (340 man-hours total)
no_benchmarks.evaluation: Manual validation of 346 tasks: 4% prompt issues, 0.6% test issues — comparable to established benchmarks

no_training_data.challenge: Very few public repos (400 MoonBit, 2,900 Gleam with ≥10 stars)

solution_modules.effectiveness: Modest improvement; best on simpler tasks

solution_modules.data_used: 28.3M (Gleam) / 13.7M (MoonBit) tokens — all available code and docs

no_instruction_following_after_pre_train.challenge: Base models pre-trained on language lack instruction-following capabilities
no_instruction_following_after_pre_train.solution_module: Instruction transferring via weight diff: Δw = Mi_weights − Mb_weights → apply to Mbk
no_instruction_following_after_pre_train.effectiveness: +12% average improvement over pre-training alone; enables 8B model to beat 32B

syntactic_errors.challenge: ~2/3 of failures in no-resource languages are syntactic
syntactic_errors.solution_module: Pre-training + instruction transferring reduces syntactic errors to <20% (large model) or shifts to semantic errors with net gain (small model)
syntactic_errors.alternative_not_explored: Constrained decoding (e.g., XGrammar) could enforce grammar at inference time

reconstructed_problem_statement: Given the practical need for code generation support in proprietary/no-resource programming languages, and given the complete failure of current LLMs on these languages (0-1% pass@1 on hard tasks), how can companies deploy cost-effective, instruction-following code generation assistants for such languages using only the minimal available data (<3K GitHub repos) and without access to proprietary instruction-tuning datasets or expensive compute?
key_insights: The resource hierarchy is not a smooth gradient — there's a discontinuity between low-resource and no-resource (0-1% vs 27-84%).
Pre-training exploits data more efficiently than fine-tuning by using all code/docs, not just paired examples.
Weight diff transfer is a 'free lunch' — it adds instruction-following at negligible cost.
Small models + smart transfer can beat large models + full training — important for resource-constrained companies.
Syntactic failure is the signature of no-resource languages — fixing this is the key bottleneck.
MoonBit's AI-friendly design (per its authors) may amplify technique effectiveness — language design and AI supportability may be coupled.

strengths: Systematic comparison: same models used across all techniques
Statistical rigor: McNemar test with Benjamini-Hochberg correction
Reproducible: all benchmarks, code, and data publicly released
Practical focus: techniques chosen for real-world company deployment feasibility
limitations: Function-level benchmarks only — not real-world tasks like debugging or refactoring
Data contamination cannot be fully ruled out for open models
Translation pipeline may introduce bias — benchmarks are translations, not native
